If I have a problem which I have no idea about, I have a step by step solution to solve the problem. The first thing I do is to understand the problem by reading it carefully and understanding what I do not understand. Then, I research the problem by using reliable sources, like online tutorials, documents, or videos, to get a basic understanding of the problem. After this, I try to solve the problem by applying what I have learned. If I have no idea, I try to solve the problem with the help of others, like my classmates, because different people have different ways of understanding things, which may help me understand the problem. Then, I read the solution again to understand what I did wrong and what I have learned. After this, I practice problems related to the problem I have solved to have a better understanding. This helps me to be patient while learning and solving problems, which is important in learning and problem solving.

First I try to understand what the problem is .If I don't understand it at all , I will search on internet or ask Ai to understand the problems .

first I will try to understand what the problem is deep understanding like I will try to research everything then if i get the problem I will just have to put everything I have learned on solving that problem
If I face a problem I don’t understand at all, I first try to clearly define it and break it down into smaller parts to identify what exactly is unclear. Then, I do initial research using reliable sources such as documentation, tutorials, and forums. Next, I try to reproduce the problem in a controlled environment and experiment with possible solutions. After that, I carefully analyze any error messages or outputs to gain more insight. I also compare my approach with similar examples to see what I might be missing. If I am still stuck, I reach out for help from more experienced people or communities, making sure to explain what I have already tried. Finally, once I solve the problem, I review the solution to fully understand it and learn from the experience.
If I face a problem I don’t understand at all, I start by breaking it down into smaller parts and identifying what I do know. Then I research using reliable resources and try to understand the basic concepts first. I experiment and test possible solutions, even if I make mistakes. If I’m still stuck, I seek help from others or communities. Most importantly, I stay patient and keep trying until I find a solution
First, I will carefully read and try to understand the problem, even if it seems confusing. Then I will break it into smaller parts to make it easier to analyze. I will research the topic using reliable resources like documentation, videos, or examples. I will try different approaches and test possible solutions. If I’m still stuck, I will ask for help from others or discuss it with peers. Finally, once I solve it, I will review the solution to fully understand it and avoid the same confusion in the future.
First, I try to break the problem into smaller parts to understand it better. Then, I research online, check tutorials or documentation, and experiment with small tests. If I’m still stuck, I ask for guidance from someone experienced. I make sure to stay patient and keep learning step by step until I understand it.

10. Imagine you try something Over 10 times and it keeps failing Even if you tried for Hours. What will you do next?100 responses
there is nothing i can't solve with in 10 times of trial
If something keeps failing, I take a break, review my steps, and try a new approach. I stay patient and learn from each attempt until I solve it.
If something fails over 10 times, I don’t just repeat the same approach. I pause, analyze what’s going wrong, and change my strategy. I break the problem into smaller parts, research new solutions, or ask for help. I stay patient, learn from mistakes, and keep improving until I succeed.
i will try for 11.
I will try 100 times if it is needed and check the procedures
I tried to figure out the mistake and try to change my approch keep until i do
If something keeps failing even after many attempts, I don’t just keep repeating the same approach. Instead, I step back and reassess the situation. First, I take a short break to clear my mind and avoid frustration. Then I review everything I’ve done to identify patterns or mistakes I might have missed. I try a different strategy—such as simplifying the problem, testing each part separately, or changing my tools or method.
I will refer different documentation and source that might help me to accomplish the task, plus as a programmer I have a habit to be more patient on such kind of problem
I go back and check my steps
If I fail many times, I don’t just repeat the same way. I stop and check what I did wrong. Then I try a different approach or learn the concept again. If needed, I take a short break and come back with a fresh mind. I believe failure helps me learn better.
By repeatedly trying to solve the problem
I will try to identify the cause of the repetitive failure if the problem is from my approach I will change how I pursue things if it's because am not well educated about it I will give myself time to relearn everything first but if I am still stuck I will definitely ask for help from those who have more experience.
Just tolerate and try it again and again But if i am in the wrong way like what i say before i will ask the one who have better experience than me
If something keeps failing even after many attempts, I take a short break and come back with a fresh perspective. Then I review my approach to find what I might be missing and try a different strategy. If needed, I look for guidance from reliable sources or ask for help to understand the problem better. I stay patient and keep improving my approach instead of giving up.
I take a break to clear my mind, then come back and analyze what I might be doing wrong. I try a different method or look for new explanations. I see failure as part of learning, so I use it to improve instead of giving up.
If I try something repeatedly and it keeps failing, I first stop and analyze why it’s failing. I ask myself what could be causing the problem and adjust my approach instead of repeating the same method blindly. If I’m still stuck after persistent attempts, I seek guidance from a professional or someone experienced to get a fresh perspective, then apply what I learned to solve the problem
as i said before when i was practicing solving different problems by cpp i used to fail a lot cause i was all by myself so i actually get pretty good experience of keeping up while failing repetitively but i will not be so arrogant i would try to ask someone or may be even use AI or some guidance but i wont be100 percent dependent i actually love doing things my way but i could use a little help
I would take a short break and then review what I did step by step to find the mistake. I would also look for other resources or ask for guidance, then try again with a different approach.
I will try for 11th time
If something keeps failing even after many attempts, I don’t give up immediately. First, I take a short break to clear my mind, then I review my approach to see if I’m repeating the same mistake. I try a different strategy or simplify the problem. If it still doesn’t work, I look for reliable resources or ask for help to gain a new perspective. I see repeated failure as part of the learning process, and I use it to improve rather than get discouraged.
Instead of giving up, I treat repeated failure as feedback, not defeat. After several failed attempts, I step back to identify what I haven't yet tried ,because continuing the same approach will only produce the same result. I take a short break to reset my mindset, then review each attempt to find patterns or assumptions that might be flawed. I also seek a fresh perspective by asking someone else or researching alternative methods. For me, failure is not end of something but the lesson that I dive deep into it. I learn from what didn't work, adjust my approach, and try again with a clearer strategy. Persistence combined with reflection is how I eventually turn failure into success.
If I fail repeatedly, I step back, analyze what’s not working, try a different approach, and seek help if needed instead of repeating the same mistake.
When I face repeated failure even after trying many times I shift my mindset and strategy instead of just pushing harder. how i respond step back, Reframe the problem, change perspective, Seek feedback breakdown into smaller
If something keeps failing even after many tries, I would take a short break to clear my mind and avoid frustration. Then I would review my approach to see if I’m repeating the same mistake. After that, I would look for new ways to solve the problem, such as checking different resources or asking for help from others. I believe failure is part of learning, so I stay patient and keep trying with a better strategy instead of giving up.
If i feel the way i am taking is wrong i will try to change butbif i feel it's right i will continue to work until it succeed
If I were in that position, here is the strategic "circuit breaker" I would use to move forward: The Hard Reset (Step Away) The most productive thing you can do after 10 failures is nothing. Walk away for at least 30 minutes. Physical movement (a walk, grabbing coffee, or even a quick workout) breaks the "tunnel vision" that happens during repetitive failure. Your subconscious often finds the solution when you stop staring at the problem.
I will step back, review my approach, try a different method, and seek help if needed. I see failure as part of learning.
I try to understand what i am missing from the very first moment and then try to solve again
I will respond to repeated failure by: by documenting what I have tried, re-reading the document, asking for help from peer and changing Methodology (e.g breaking down into smaller part..)..etc
For me repeated failure is not a reason to stop but a sign that I need to adjust my approach and learn something new so I review what i have already tried and look for patterns in the failures then I change my strategy
If something keeps failing even after many attempts, I don’t give up. First, I take a short break to clear my mind. Then I review what I’ve tried to find mistakes or patterns. I try a different approach or method, and if needed, I seek help from others or look for new resources. I stay patient and keep learning until I find a solution.
Take a break and try to see what went wrong in a relaxed mind
I try until I get it
If I fail many times, I don’t just repeat the same approach. First, I step back and analyze what I might be doing wrong. I break the problem into smaller parts and test each part to find the exact issue. I also look for other resources such as tutorials, documentation, or examples to understand different approaches. If needed, I ask for help or feedback from others. After that, I try again with a better strategy. I believe repeated failure is part of learning, and each attempt helps me improve until I succeed.
If it fails over and over, even after many tries, I will: - Stop and check what’s going wrong. - Look for a different approach or solution. - Ask for help or advice. - Try again in smaller steps. - If it still doesn't work, I will move on and learn a new method.
1. Recheck the program deeply 2. Take a break and back 3. I will stay persistent and re think it 4. I will approach otheer and ask what is wrong with my program 5.
If I try something many times and it keeps failing, I would not continue repeating the same approach. Instead, I would take a step back and review what I have already tried to identify possible mistakes or patterns. Next, I would break the problem down again and approach it from a different angle. I might revisit the fundamentals to make sure I fully understand the concept or simplify the problem to isolate where the issue is coming from. If I am still stuck, I would use other resources such as documentation, tutorials, or discussions from online communities to gain new perspectives. I would also be open to asking for help.
If I try something over 10 times and it still doesn’t work, I won’t just keep doing the same thing again and again. That usually means I’m missing something. First, I take a short break to clear my head. After that, I come back and look at the problem from the beginning, step by step, to see where I might be going wrong. Then I change my approach. I might simplify the problem, test smaller parts, or try a completely different method. If I’ve been stuck for a long time, I’ll also check online resources or ask someone for help. Most importantly, I don’t see failure as a bad thing. It helps me understand what doesn’t work and pushes me to improve. I stay patient, adjust my strategy, and keep trying until I figure it out.
If I keep failing, I will try a different method and look for new approaches. I will also review what I did wrong, learn from my mistakes, and continue trying until I succeed.
If something keeps failing after many attempts, I take a short break to clear my mind and avoid frustration. Then I review my approach step by step to find mistakes and try a different method. If needed, I look for guidance from reliable resources or ask others for help. I stay patient, learn from the failure, and keep improving until I find a solution.
Patience
If something keeps failing I analyze what went wrong and adjust my approach instead of repeating the same steps I test different solutions learn from each attempt and stay patient If needed, I take a short break and return with a clearer perspective but I remain persistent until I find a solution
I must calm down and ask guide from my seniors I think ot would be great in this way
I try something first i ask my freind that have more skill than me.if it doesn't work .I ask ai assistance to solve this problem then i solve the problem.
I would take a step back and walk away from the screen for a short break to reset my perspective. Often, repeated failure is a sign that I am stuck in a 'tunnel vision' loop. When I return, I would change my strategy entirely rather than repeating the same steps. I would verify my initial assumptions, check for small errors I might have overlooked, and perhaps search for how others have solved similar issues to see if there is a different methodology I hadn't considered.
I try it again until i achieve
Also trying again after analyzing my previous trials and what was the the mistakes or misunderstandings there and by correcting them and changing mechanisms based on the anaylsis trying again
If I try something many times and it continues to fail even after spending hours on it, I first pause and reassess my approach instead of repeating the same method. I review each step I took to identify where the mistake or misunderstanding might be and check whether I clearly understood the problem requirements. Next, I break the problem into smaller parts and test each part separately to find exactly where the failure occurs. I also look for new learning resources, documentation, or examples to gain a different perspective, because repeated failure often means my current understanding is incomplete. If the issue still remains, I seek guidance from others by asking questions in learning communities, discussing with peers, or consulting someone with more experience. I explain what I have already tried so I can receive more targeted advice. Most importantly, I stay patient and treat failure as part of the learning process rather than a reason to give up. After finding the solution, I reflect on what went wrong and document the lesson so I can avoid the same mistake in the future. Repeated challenges motivate me to improve my skills, persistence, and problem-solving mindset.
know
If something keeps failing even after many tries, I don’t just repeat the same thing again and again. First, I stop for a moment and think about what I did before. I try to see if there is a pattern or small mistake I didn’t notice. I go through my previous attempts to understand what went wrong. After that, I try a different way. Maybe I simplify the problem or break it into smaller parts so it’s easier to handle. I also search more and look at similar problems to get new ideas. If I still can’t solve it, I’m okay to ask others for help, but I explain what I already tried so I can get better advice. For me, failing many times is not a bad thing, it actually helps me learn and improve until I finally find the solution.
I’d realize I’m just going in circles. So I’d stop, take a break, maybe clear my head a bit. Then I’d come back and look at it fresh, like “okay, what am I missing here?” If it still doesn’t work, I’d try a totally different way or go back to the basics. And if I’m still stuck, I’d just ask someone because sometimes you really can’t see the mistake on your own.
May be I need some help
Honestly If I tried something over 10 times and it kept failing even after hours I would probably get frustrated. I won't lie. There is a moment where I would want to just walk away But I wouldn't. I'd step back first take a breather, drink some water, clear my head. Because when I'm frustrated I'm not thinking straight. I'm just repeating the same thing expecting different results. After that, I would come back and look at it fresh. I would ask myself . Wait, am I even going about this the right way? Maybe I missed something simple. Maybe I need to go back to the beginning and understand it again from scratch.If I'm still stuck, I'd reach out. I'd ask someone who knows more than me, or search for answers until I find something that clicks.And if it still fails I'd remind myself that this is part of the process. It doesn't mean I'm not good enough it just means I haven't figured it out yet. I'd take what I learned and try again.I don't quit. I crave real challenges, and real challenges don't come easy. That's the whole point."
Failure is an example of trying something new. Invaluable discoveries are made through many failures and trials. I believe that if I fail repeatedly, it means I am attempting to do something worthwhile.
I pause and rethink my approach instead of repeating the same method. I simplify the problem, try a new method, consult resources, or ask for guidance. I see failure as feedback, not defeat.
If I try many times and keep failing, I try a different approach, ask for help if needed, and take a short break to think clearly. I see repeated failure as a chance to learn and improve, not as a reason to quit.
review my approach and try a different method. If needed I will seek help and keep trying until I improve.
“If something keeps failing after many tries, I take a short break, review my work, search for new solutions, and ask for help if needed. I don’t give up easily and keep learning from mistakes.”
If something keeps failing even after many attempts, I step back instead of forcing it. I review what I’ve already tried, look for patterns in the mistakes, and rethink my approach from a different angle.
I want to commit myself untill it works, through consistency and hope that this is the process. Also want to understand the main problem why it is failing, not repeating the same thing many time, changing my approach untill finally get what i want .And also communicate with those who are more skilled than me in the technology because everyone passed in the process im facing
After ten failures, I take a break to reset my focus and prevent frustration. When I return, I re-examine my initial assumptions and deconstruct the process to identify hidden logic errors rather than repeating the same approach. If I’m still stuck, I change my strategy entirely or seek a second opinion to gain the fresh perspective needed to break the cycle.
taking break and redo
If I try something many times and it keeps failing, I will first take a short break to refresh my mind. Then, I will review my approach carefully to identify mistakes or missing steps. I may try a different method or strategy instead of repeating the same one. I will also seek help from others or check reliable resources for new ideas. I see failure as a learning process, so I stay patient and keep improving until I find a solution.
If I try something many times and it keeps failing, I don’t just keep repeating the same approach. Instead, I pause and change my strategy. First, I take a short break to clear my mind. Working for hours can make me tired and less effective, so stepping away helps me come back with a fresh perspective. Next, I review everything I’ve done and look for patterns in my mistakes. I ask myself what might be going wrong and whether I misunderstood a concept or used the wrong method. Then, I try a different approach. This could mean using another method, simplifying the problem, or starting again from the basics. If needed, I look for new resources like videos, books, or examples that explain the problem differently. If I’m still stuck, I ask for help from someone more experienced, such as a teacher or a friend. I make sure to explain what I’ve already tried so I can get more specific guidance. Most importantly, I stay patient and don’t get discouraged. I see repeated failure as part of the learning process. Each attempt teaches me something new, and eventually, it helps me succeed.
If something keeps failing after many attempts, I would not keep repeating the exact same approach without reflection. First, I would pause and review everything I have done to identify patterns in the failure and understand what might be going wrong. Then I would simplify the problem, test smaller parts, and compare my approach with trusted resources or examples. If I still cannot solve it, I would ask for guidance from someone more experienced so I can learn the correct direction instead of wasting time. I see repeated failure not as a reason to quit, but as a sign that I need a better strategy, more patience, and a deeper understanding of the problem.
Continued again and again
i will continue as long as i get the result
Trying again
I'll take a deep breath and try again
If something keeps failing after many attempts, I usually take a short break to clear my mind, then come back and look at the problem again with a fresh perspective. I try to rethink my approach instead of repeating the same steps, and I check if I misunderstood anything. If needed, I break the problem into smaller parts or try a different method. If I’m still stuck, I look at documentation or ask for help while explaining what I’ve already tried.
When faced with repeated failure after multiple attempts, I follow a 'Reset and Review' strategy: Step Away: I take a short break to clear my head. Often, a fresh perspective reveals a simple error I missed while frustrated. Verify Assumptions: I go back to the very beginning and double-check my basic assumptions. Usually, repeated failure means I am building on a small, hidden mistake at the start. Change the Variable: Instead of trying the same solution an 11th time, I change one specific part of my approach to see if the outcome shifts. Collaborate: If I am still stuck, I explain the problem to a peer. Describing the failure out loud often helps me 'see' the solution myself, or they may offer a tool I hadn't considered.
If something fails even after many tries, I don’t see it as failure—I see it as feedback. I step back, analyze what’s not working, adjust my approach, and try smarter. And if needed, I seek advice, because persistence with learning always leads to progress.
I stop repeating the same approach and take a step back. I review the basics, look for what I might be missing, and try a different strategy. If needed, I take a short break or ask for help.
I would step away from the screen for a short break to clear my head. Often, 'tunnel vision' prevents us from seeing obvious mistakes. When I return, I would start from scratch with a new perspective, questioning my initial assumptions. In cybersecurity, failure is just a data point that tells you which path doesn't work, bringing you closer to the one that does.
Everything needs patience whatever it takes try again is the solution
If something keeps failing, I take a step back, review what I’ve done, identify what’s going wrong, and try a new approach. I stay patient, learn from each attempt, and keep adjusting until I find a solution.
If something keeps failing even after many attempts, I don’t just keep repeating the same approach. First, I take a short break to clear my mind and avoid frustration. Then I review what I’ve already tried and identify possible mistakes or patterns. I look for new methods by researching or learning from different sources instead of repeating the same steps. If I’m still stuck, I ask for help from someone more experienced, like a teacher or online community. After getting new insight, I try again with a better strategy. Overall, I stay patient and persistent, but I also adapt my approach instead of giving up or repeating the same error.
I will stop repeating the same approach and analyze my mistakes. Then I’ll try a different method, use new resources, and come back with a clearer strategy.
I go to someone who is better than me in skill
If something keeps failing after many attempts, I would: 1,Take a short break to clear my mind and avoid frustration. 2,Review my approach to see if I’m repeating the same mistake. 3,Break the problem down differently and try a new strategy. 4,Look for other resources (tutorials, examples, documentation). 5,Ask for help from someone more experienced. 6,Test step by step to find exactly where things go wrong. I see repeated failure as feedback, not defeat—it helps me adjust and improve until I succeed.
Amazing question, I will ask people that have knowledge about it and study with my friends. For example, When I was 10 grade student and I studied maths about polynomial with my friend and we started @8:00LT one question was very difficult for us. Even after trying many times, we couldn’t solve it at first. Instead of giving up, we kept discussing, rechecking our steps, and trying different approaches. After about two hours of continuous effort, we finally solved it at 10:00 LT. This experience taught me that repeated failure is part of learning. When I face challenges, I stay patient, seek help, and try new methods until I succeed.
If the problem is not out off my effort i will add my attention and I try to solve the problem by giving good attention and effort
If I fail many times, I don’t give up. Instead, I change my approach.
If something keeps failing ,I don't think it as wasted effort but as feedback because i know one way things won't work , first I review what I've already tried and identify mistakes , I will try another method and try to debug and test smaller part to know where the issue is , and I will use some other resources, until I get successed , but if this don't help I'm open to ask other
If I do something over 10 times without any results, I do not stop. I change my strategy First of all I take a break to refresh my mind like taking a nap or watching some motivational videos. Then I try to evaluate what I have been doing to see if there is any mistakes or patterns to my failures then I try to do it another way rather than doing the same thing over and over again. I also try to find resources or ask someone to help me. I have learned that failures are repetitive, which are helpful to me. I take my failures as a way to learn until I grasp or comprehend.
I can do to things- 1,First I focus on understanding the problem deeply without proceeding to solution to make sure that the solution steps I am taking can really solve the problem. 2 , I change my approach to solve the problem
Still keep doing with alternative ways.
I really don't know the answer for that I don't think I have patience for things but that doesn't mean I will quit everything right away as long as I remember i don't think I have ever experienced that much failure at one thing or may I just didn't even try it but I want to test that part of me if you allow me through this club I Hope I can see my ability to go through things that are hard
If something keeps failing after many attempts, I step back and reassess my approach instead of repeating the same steps. I review the fundamentals, check reliable resources, and try a different method or simplify the problem. If I’m still stuck, I ask for guidance while explaining what I’ve already tried. I see repeated failure as part of learning, so I stay patient and keep improving until I solve it.
If something keeps failing even after many attempts, I won’t just repeat the same approach. I would step back, analyze what I’ve tried, and identify where things might be going wrong. Then I’d research new methods, learn from reliable sources, or ask for guidance if needed. I’d return with a different strategy and keep improving until I find a solution
If something keeps failing after many attempts, I won’t just repeat the same approach. Instead, I will step back and analyze what I might be doing wrong. I will review my process, look for patterns in the mistakes, and try a different strategy. I may also take a short break to clear my mind and come back with a fresh perspective. If needed, I will seek guidance or study similar problems to understand a better method. Failure doesn’t discourage me—it helps me learn. I believe that repeated failure is part of mastering difficult skills, especially in fields like cybersecurity.
I stay calm and take a short break to clear my mind. Then, I review what I’ve done carefully to spot mistakes or patterns I missed. I try a new approach, or seek advice if needed. I see failure as a learning opportunity, so I keep experimenting until I find a solution.
Try again
If I fail repeatedly I'll ask for help and if that didn't work I'll take my mind off the challenge and refresh and try again, and to cheer up I'll keep reminding my self that failure is a great teacher.
Continue again to again
If something keeps failing after many tries, I usually start to feel frustrated, so I take a time break to clear my mind. Then I come back and carefully check what I might be doing wrong step by step. If I still can’t solve it, I look for simpler explanations or different tutorials. Sometimes I ask others or online communities for help. If it’s still not working, I pause and come back to it later with a fresh mind. I don’t always solve it immediately, but I try not to give up and keep learning little by little.
When I try something many times and it keeps failing, I don’t just continue repeating the same approach. First, I take a short break to clear my mind so I can come back with a fresh perspective. Then I carefully review what I’ve been doing to identify patterns in my mistakes or where things might be going wrong. Next, I change my strategy—this could mean trying a different method, simplifying the problem, or breaking it into smaller parts. I also look for new resources such as documentation, examples, or tutorials to see alternative approaches. If I’m still unable to solve it, I ask for help from others, such as classmates, instructors, or online communities, because getting a different perspective can reveal what I might be missing. Finally, once I solve the problem, I reflect on what went wrong and what worked, so I can avoid the same mistakes in the future. I see repeated failure not as a stopping point, but as a learning process that helps me improve my problem-solving skills.”---

Section 5: Discipline & Focus
11. If this becomes very difficult and slow, will you still continue? Why?102 responses
i will coz i will continue
Yes
Yes, I will continue because I know patience and persistence help me learn and reach my goals.
Yes, I will continue. Difficult and slow progress means I’m learning something valuable. I focus on consistency rather than speed, breaking the challenge into smaller steps and improving gradually. I stay motivated by my long-term goals, knowing that persistence and patience are what turn difficult tasks into real skills and meaningful success.
Yes
ya i will continue because if i start something i have to finish.
Yeah i will continue because if i put my mind in to it i can do anything
Yes beacause i belive progess makes me perfect
Yes, I will continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. I believe real learning takes time, especially in complex fields like cybersecurity. Challenges and slow progress are part of the process, not a sign to quit. My mindset is to stay consistent, be patient, and focus on gradual improvement rather than quick results.
yeah, I will continue because I have my own Intention to work on cybersecurity.
Yes, I will continue. I understand that learning something like cybersecurity takes time and patience. Even if it is slow, I am willing to keep going because I am interested in it and I want to improve myself step by step.
I will continue because after big challenges there's big reward
Depends on what matter the most at the moment if the thing I am working on is very much important it is fine to take your time learning and exploring your weaknesses but if it will drag you and the team then maybe try solving it by yourself and move to other projects.
I will try to be sure whither i am on the right way or not If i am right i will tolerate and go on until reaching the end
Yes, I will continue. I believe difficult and slow progress is still progress, and challenges help me grow and improve my skills over time. I stay motivated by focusing on my goals and reminding myself why I started. Even if it takes time, I am willing to stay consistent and keep learning until I achieve it.
yes i still continue cause i believe that Difficult and slow progress is part of mastering complex skills.
Yes, I will continue. If the problem is new and challenging for everyone, I follow the mindset of Thomas Edison, who tried 99 times before succeeding. I stay committed to solving it, especially if it has a significant impact. However, if the problem is not new and already solved by others, I believe in learning from their experience instead of struggling alone
yes, i will because i have this compulsive obsession thing that i can't let things go unless i finished them so i won't stop unless i did finish it but i am flexible i will try to use different ways but still i won't let that go without getting finished
Yes, because I believe difficult things take time to understand. I’m willing to be patient and keep learning step by step.
Yes, because I have to get what I want to
Yes, I will continue. I understand that difficult and slow progress is part of learning, especially in complex fields. I stay motivated by focusing on long-term goals and consistent improvement. Even small progress matters, and I believe persistence is key to success.
Yes, I will continue. I think that if something is worth doing, it is worth putting work into. I do not think that challenges are an indicator to stop; I think they are an indicator that I am growing. When things are not going as smoothly as I would like, I try to focus on my smaller goals. I try to remember why I am doing this and what I hope to create from this. Long-term challenges are not necessarily based on talent; they are based on showing up. As long as I am moving forward, however slow, I consider that moving forward. Stopping would only guarantee that I would not succeed.
Yes, I continue because I value long-term growth. I stay consistent, adapt my approach, and focus on progress even when things are slow or difficult.
Yes, I would still continue even if it’s very difficult and slow because I see long-term challenges as opportunities to grow. Persistence over speed Learning in the struggle Big-picture focus Adaptability
Yes, I will continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. I understand that learning something like cybersecurity takes time and consistent effort. Progress may not always be fast, but small improvements still matter. My mindset is to stay patient, keep practicing, and focus on long-term growth rather than quick results. I believe persistence is what helps overcome challenges and achieve real understanding.
Ya dreams are very slow burned so giving up is never a choice in persuasion also challenge make things brighter not dim
If a task becomes a grind, I don't just continue blindly; I shift my mindset into a long-term "marathon mode." Here is how I approach those high-friction challenges:1. The "Signal vs. Noise" Filter Before committing to a slow path, I evaluate why it’s difficult. Productive Difficulty- This is the "climb." It’s hard because the problem is complex, but every small step provides a new piece of data. I will absolutely continue here because the struggle is where the expertise is built. Unproductive Difficulty: This is "spinning wheels." It’s hard because the tools are wrong or the goal is ill-defined. In this case, I don't just "continue"—I stop and pivot.
Yes, because I understand progress takes time. I’m committed to learning and improving even when it’s challenging.
If i ever started something that i believe it gonna have something special for my future i won't stop . I'm not that kind of person who just give up in tough moments . I have no experience in giving up
Yes, I will continue. Because Challenges are just learning opportunities rather than reason to stop.
Since long term challenges build discipline, patience, and real understanding i am willing to stay committed and keep improving step by step so yes i will continue
Yes, I will continue. I understand that difficult and slow progress is part of learning, especially with complex fields like cybersecurity. I stay patient and focus on long-term goals rather than quick results. Challenges help me grow, improve my skills, and build persistence, so I see them as opportunities to learn rather than reasons to quit.
Yes, as I mentioned above im in this field because im in love with it
I will if it helps me
Yes, I will continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. I understand that learning cybersecurity takes time, patience, and consistent effort. Challenges are part of the process, and they help build strong skills and deeper understanding. I stay motivated by focusing on my long-term goal of becoming skilled in cybersecurity. Even small progress matters to me, and I am willing to stay disciplined and keep learning until I improve.
Yes, I’ll continue. As a programmer, I stay calm and break the problem into smaller parts, test often, and keep learning from failures. Long tasks are normal—I focus on progress, not speed.
When facing long-term challenges, I focus on growth, stay active as much as possible, persistence, and take challengesas opportunity. Celebrating small victories and seeking support helps maintain motivation and resilience throughout the journey.
Yes, I will. I understand that mastering cybersecurity is not something that happens quickly, and real progress takes time, effort, and consistency. My mindset toward long-term challenges is to focus on steady improvement rather than immediate results. Even if progress feels slow, I believe that every small step builds a stronger foundation over time. Difficult moments are part of the learning process, and they often lead to deeper understanding. I am motivated by my long-term goal of building a career in technology, and I know that persistence and discipline are necessary to achieve it. Instead of giving up when things get hard. Because of this, I am committed to staying focused, being patient, and continuing to learn even when the process is challenging.
Yes, I would continue even if it becomes very difficult and slow. I understand that meaningful goals, especially in fields like cybersecurity or programming, take time and consistent effort. Progress is not always fast, and there are times when learning feels slow or frustrating. However, I believe that steady improvement is more important than quick results. My mindset is to focus on long-term growth rather than short-term comfort. Even small progress each day adds up over time. Difficult challenges also help me build patience, discipline, and deeper understanding, which are essential for mastering complex skills. What keeps me going is my goal and interest in the field. I remind myself why I started and what I want to achieve. Instead of giving up when things get hard, I adjust my pace if needed, but I don’t stop. So yes, even if the journey is slow, I’m willing to stay consistent and committed because I know the results will be worth it in the long run.
Yes, I will continue because I don’t want to lose hope in my life. I believe success takes time and patience.
Yes, I will continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. I believe real learning takes time and consistency. Challenges help me grow, improve my skills, and build patience. I stay focused on my long-term goal in cybersecurity, which motivates me to keep going despite difficulties
I stop forcing it I will take a break and go back to basics make it smaller break it down switch perspective then try again and again
Yes i will continue even if it becames difficult. I belive long term sucess comes from consistency
I take it curious
Yes I stay consistent even when progress is slow because I focus on long-term growth and results.
There is no success without a hard time it is the part of the journey, so I will try my best to succeed no backing off
I think yes i will try to accept there is
Yes, I will continue. My mindset toward long-term challenges is that the 'slow' periods are usually where the deepest learning happens. In engineering, mastery isn't about quick wins; it's about the discipline to keep showing up when things are tough. I view difficulties as a necessary part of the growth process, and my commitment is fueled by the end goal of becoming a proficient specialist.
Yes i do, because i enjoy this path even if if hard
Yeah I will try my best
Yes, I will continue even if the process becomes very difficult and slow. I believe that meaningful learning and real skill development take time, patience, and consistent effort. Difficult challenges are often a sign that I am learning something new and growing beyond my current abilities. My mindset toward long-term challenges is to focus on progress rather than speed. Even small improvements each day bring me closer to my goal. When learning becomes slow, I remind myself of the reason I started and the long-term impact the skills will have on my future and on helping others. I also understand that setbacks and delays are part of any learning journey, especially in complex fields like cybersecurity. Instead of giving up, I adjust my learning strategies, take breaks when needed, and continue with renewed focus. Persistence, discipline, and continuous learning are values I rely on to overcome long-term challenges and achieve lasting success.
no i will take other chance
Yes, I will continue. I believe that meaningful progress often comes from working through difficult and slow challenges. Even if the process is frustrating, I focus on long term growth rather than quick results. I stay motivated by reminding myself of my goal and the skills I am building along the way. I also break the work into smaller steps to make progress more manageable and maintain consistency. Difficult situations teach patience, discipline, and resilience. For me, continuing despite challenges is important because it strengthens my ability to handle complex problems and prepares me for bigger opportunities in the future.
Yeah, I would continuebut not in a blind, exhausting way. If something is really difficult and slow, I’d remind myself that it’s supposed to feel that way. Hard things take time, and struggling usually means I’m actually learning. But I wouldn’t just push nonstop. I’d pace myself, take breaks, and focus on small progress instead of expecting quick results. Even tiny improvement is still progress.
Yeah, I'll be honest if it gets very difficult and slow, I won't pretend I'll be happy about it. There will probably be days where I feel stuck, where I question if I'm even good enough, where I wonder if I'm wasting my time. But will I continue? Yes. I know that about myself. Here's why I didn't start this because I thought it would be easy. I started because I wanted something that would actually push me. I crave real challenges. If it was easy and fast, I'd probably get bored and move on to something else anyway. For me, the hard and slow part that's the real test. Anyone can do something when it's exciting and new. But the people who actually make it are the ones who stay when it's frustrating, when they feel like they're not making progress.I want to be that person. Also, I know that anything worth building takes time. I don't expect to become good at cybersecurity in a few months. I know it's a long game. And honestly I like that. I want something that will keep me growing for years, not something I can finish quickly and check off a list. So yeah. When it gets hard and slow, I'll probably complain a little to myself I won't lie. But I won't stop. That's just not who I am.
Sometimes everything doesn't go as we deserve and want. We face challenges and struggles, but what matters is how we stand against them through dedication and commitment.
Yes, I will continue because I know mastery takes time and patience, and I’m motivated by long-term growth.
Yes, I will continue even if it becomes very difficult and slow, because I believe difficult things take time and patience. I understand that real learning and success do not come quickly, so I focus on improving step by step. I stay motivated by remembering my goals and why I started, and I am willing to work hard and be patient to achieve long-term success.
Yes Challenges are part of learning and growth. I will stay patient and keep trying until I improve and additionally the club is most important in my life because it's related with my department generally i have fully interested
“Yes, I will continue because difficult tasks help me learn and grow. Even if progress is slow, I stay patient and keep working until I succeed.”
Yes I focus on consistency rather than speed making small progress over time instead of expecting quick results. Even when it gets frustrating, I remind myself why I started and what I want to achieve.
I now things will not go as planned but i believe that it is they way of achieving it so i will challenge myself to accept slow and difficult process. I will continue because what driving me is not the outcome, it is the interest inside men I hope the feeling will pushh me through these challenges
Yes I would continue because I view slow progress as a necessary phase of growth rather than a reason to quit. My mindset is focused on the long-term value of the goal, understanding that the most significant breakthroughs often require the most persistence. yes, because i know genius people usually say try and try become perfect
Yes, I will continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. I believe that long-term challenges help build strong skills and experience. As an Information Science student, I understand that learning complex topics takes time and patience.
Yes, I would continue, even if it becomes difficult and slow. I understand that meaningful learning and real progress often take time. When something is challenging, it usually means I am growing and pushing beyond my current limits. Instead of seeing slow progress as failure, I see it as part of the process. My mindset is to focus on consistency rather than speed. Even small steps forward each day can lead to big results over time. I try to stay patient, set short-term goals, and track my improvement to stay motivated. Of course, I also make sure I’m not just struggling blindly. If progress is too slow, I adjust my approach, find better resources, or ask for help. This way, I keep improving instead of getting stuck. In the long term, persistence is what makes the difference. I believe that if I stay committed and keep learning, I will eventually overcome the challenge and achieve my goal.
Yes, I will continue even if the process becomes difficult and slow. I understand that learning a field like cybersecurity takes time, patience, and consistent effort, and I do not expect growth to happen quickly. My mindset is that long-term challenges are part of real learning, especially in technical fields where deep understanding matters more than speed. Even when progress feels slow, I believe staying disciplined and continuing step by step is what leads to real improvement. I am willing to keep learning because I am interested in the field and I want to build strong skills that will benefit me in the future.
yes i don't want to stop anything shortly
Try with another way
Yes of course, being patient and not giving up is my thing
Yes, I would still continue. My mindset toward long-term challenges is to stay consistent rather than looking for quick results. When something becomes difficult or slow, I treat it as part of the learning process, not a failure. I focus on breaking the problem into smaller parts, adjusting my approach, and improving step by step instead of expecting instant progress. If progress is slow, I remind myself that persistence matters more than speed. As long as I’m learning, adapting, and moving forward—even gradually—I consider it meaningful progress.
Yes, I will absolutely continue. My mindset toward long-term challenges is that difficulty is a sign of growth, not a reason to stop. In my Computer Science studies, I have learned that the most valuable skills—like mastering a new programming language or securing a complex network—take time and consistent effort. I don't look for 'quick fixes'; instead, I focus on the incremental progress I make each day. I am driven by the end goal of becoming a skilled professional who can solve real-world problems, and I understand that a slow, steady approach is often what leads to the most reliable and secure results.
No
Yes, I would continue. I believe real growth takes time, and slow progress is still progress. Even when it’s difficult, I stay consistent and remind myself why I started.
Absolutely. I understand that cybersecurity has a steep learning curve. I view long-term challenges as an investment; the harder a skill is to acquire, the more valuable it is. My goal isn't just to find easy answers, but to develop the grit required to handle real-world security threats that don't have simple solutions.

Yes, I will continue because I focus on the goal, stay patient, and see challenges as opportunities to learn and grow.
Yes, I will continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. I believe real learning takes time, especially for complex skills. Yes. I understand learning takes time. I’m focused on long-term growth, and I’m willing to stay consistent even if progress is slow.

Yes, I would continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. Cybersecurity is a field that requires patience, persistence, and continuous learning. Yes I will continue even if it becomes very hard, difficult, and slow because I understand that to made a proper learning there must be time and effort. Yes, I will continue because I see difficult and slow progress as part of mastering a skill. Long term challenges teach patience, persistence, and problem solving. Yes, I will continue even if it becomes difficult and slow because I understand that real learning takes time and persistence. Yes, I will continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. I understand that learning cybersecurity is not something that happens quickly, and real progress takes time and effort. Yes... because i believe in progress not just the result
I personally believe most of difficult and slow processes are for greater good and this mindset keeps me consistent most of the time, so I will still continue.
Yes, I would continue even if it becomes difficult and slow. From my earlier experience of learning to use Kali Linux on Oracle VM VirtualBox, I learned that progress doesn’t always happen quickly. At first, I struggled with installation issues and not understanding commands, but I used online tutorials and communities like GeezSec and ChatGPT to help me improve step by step. Even when something failed many times, I learned to take breaks, review what I did, and keep trying from different angles. I know learning cybersecurity will take time, and it might be slow sometimes, but my mindset is to stay patient and consistent because small improvements over time lead to real progress toward my goals.
Yes, I will continue—even if it becomes difficult and slow—because I understand that meaningful learning and real progress take time. When something is challenging, it usually means I am pushing beyond my current limits, which is how growth happens.

12. How many hours per day can you realistically dedicate to this club?103 responses
3
2
2hr
4
I can dedicate about 1–2 hours per day to this club.
1hr
I can realistically dedicate 2–3 hours per day to the club. I can consistently manage this alongside my studies by setting a fixed schedule. On weekends, I can extend this to 4–5 hours if needed, allowing me to stay committed, practice regularly, and contribute effectively without compromising other responsibilities.
2 hour
May be 2
When i am free from class
I can realistically dedicate 2 to 3 hours per day to the club. On regular days, I can consistently spend around 2 hours, and on weekends or less busy days, I can extend it to 3 hours or more. This allows me to stay consistent while balancing my academic responsibilities.
two to three hours
I can dedicate around 2 to 3 hours per day, depending on my schedule. I will try to stay consistent and manage my time properly.
6 upto 7
On average, I can dedicate around 3 hours per day. I’m consistent with my schedule, and I can increase that time when needed, especially during important tasks or deadlines.
As much as i can i will be there no matter how many time is it
4 hour
6hours /days
3 up to 4 hours it depends on some situation like exam season or something like that but i can manage at least 3 hours per day
Around 1–2 hours per day, depending on my class schedule and other responsibilities.
I can dedicate about 2 to 3 hours per day. I can stay consistent with this schedule while managing my other responsibilities, and I’m willing to adjust and give more time when needed.
I can realiably dedicate atmost for 1hr per day for this club.
I can realistically dedicate about 2 hours per day. On days when I have fewer commitments, I can spend more time if needed.
I can dedicate 3-4 hours per day.
As a student, I have regular classes, so I want to be realistic. I can dedicate around 2 to 3 hours per day to the club. On weekends or free days, I may be able to spend more time when needed. I believe consistency is more important than long hours, so I will make sure to use this time effectively and stay committed.
2 hours a day
About 2–3 hours per day consistently.
Since i have a lot to do other this to be honest i will spenx 4 hours a day
I want to dedicate 2-4 hours per day.
I can realistically dedicate about 2 to 3 hours per day to this club. On busy days I may do at least 1 hour, and on free days I can spend more time to stay consistent and keep improving.
2-3
I can dedicate at least 2-3 hour per day
I can dedicate about 2 hours per day realistically (for learning, practice, and club tasks).
2:30-3:00 hour
I can realistically dedicate around 1:30 to 2:00 hours per day to this club, considering my academic responsibilities and other commitments.
I can realistically dedicate about 2 to 3 hours per day to this club. On weekdays, I can consistently manage around 2 hours alongside my regular studies, and on weekends or free days, I can extend that to 3 hours or more if needed. I prefer to set a schedule that I can maintain long-term rather than overcommit and burn out. This amount of time allows me to stay consistent, practice regularly, and actively participate in club activities while balancing my other responsibilities.
1 - 2 hours per day
I can realistically dedicate about 2 to 3 hours per day to this club. I will manage my time carefully to balance my studies while staying consistent and fully committed to learning and contributing.
1hour
3hr
2-3 hours
3 - 4 hours
2 Hour
I can realistically dedicate 1 to 2 hours per day. I have organized my academic schedule to ensure that I have a consistent block of time in the evenings to focus entirely on club activities and deep learning without distractions.
2 hours
1 hour
I can realistically dedicate about 2 to 3 hours per day to this club. I plan to study consistently during my free time outside of my regular responsibilities, focusing on learning, practice exercises, and participating in club activities. On weekends or days with fewer commitments, I may be able to spend additional time reviewing materials and improving my skills. This schedule allows me to stay consistent while maintaining balance with my other duties.
1/2 hour
I can dedicate 2 hours daily. I am committed to prioritizing this club alongside my academic responsibilities.
2 or 3
Realistically, I can dedicate 2 to 3 hours per day. On weekends, I can do a bit more maybe 3 to 4 hours. I want to be consistent and honest about what I can actually give
I can realistically dedicate 3–5 hours per day consistently, with more time on weekends if needed.
4 to 5 hours/ week.
i can dedicate 2–3 hours per day consistently.
can dedicate around 3 to 4 hours per day to this club. I plan to organize my daily schedule so I can attend activities, prctice skills, and complete tasks on time.”
I can realistically dedicate around 2–3 hours per day. On lighter days it might be closer to 2 hours, and on weekends or free days I can extend it if needed.
In a day, i hope i will commit 1-2 hour
I can realistically dedicate 2–3 hours daily to the club. This schedule allows me to balance my studies while contributing consistently to our projects. For major events or specific deadlines, I am also happy to put in extra time on weekends.
I can realistically dedicate about 2 to 3 hours per day to this club. I will manage my time by balancing my academic responsibilities and personal activities, ensuring that I stay consistent and productive
I can realistically dedicate 2 to 3 hours per day to this club. I have planned my schedule to make sure I can stay consistent without affecting my other responsibilities. On regular days, I can commit around 2 hours for learning, practicing, and completing tasks. On weekends or less busy days, I can increase this to about 3–4 hours if needed. I believe consistency is more important than occasional long hours, so I will focus on showing up every day and making steady progress.
I can realistically dedicate around 2 to 3 hours per day to this club, depending on my academic schedule. On less busy days or weekends, I can give even more time when needed. I want to be honest and realistic because I believe consistency is more important than giving an unrealistic answer.
1
4hr
I can realistically dedicate 1.5 hour to 2 hours per day (approximately 10–12 hours per week) to the club. As a second-year Computer Science student, I have a demanding academic schedule, but I prioritize my growth in cybersecurity. I prefer to commit to a consistent daily block of time rather than irregular hours, as this allows me to make steady progress on club projects and stay updated on new security threats without compromising my studies.
I can dedicate 3-4 hours per day
I can realistically dedicate 2 hours per day on weekdays and more during the weekends. I prefer consistent, daily progress over cramming.
Morethan 3 hr
I can dedicate 1:30 up to 2 hours per day
I can realistically dedicate 1:00–1:30 hours per day to this club. I can use this time for learning, practicing skills, and participating in club activities without affecting my other responsibilities.
I can dedicate 2–3 hours per day consistently.
When i don't have class i will be there
I can realistically dedicate 2–3 hours per day to the club, depending on my schedule.
2 hours and always except from 11:00-1:00LT(my education or class time).
can realistically dedicate about 2 to 3 hours per day.

may be 1 to 2 hours
I can realistically dedicate 1 to 2 hours per day to the club on weekdays, and 3 to 4 hours per day on weekends. I want to balance my regular schoolwork while consistently improving my cybersecurity skills.
2–4 hours per day
can realistically dedicate around 2 to 3 hours per day to this club. I chose this time because it allows me to stay consistent without affecting my other responsibilities. During this time, I will focus on learning, practicing, and improving my skills step by step.
I can realistically dedicate 2–3 hours per day to the club, and more on weekends if needed, to fully engage and learn.
2hr
I believe I can dedicate 2 hours per day on work days and 3-4hours per day on weekend.
3 hours
I can realistically dedicate about 2 to 3 hours per day to this club. I’ve considered my class schedule and other responsibilities, and this amount of time allows me to stay consistent without affecting my studies.

SPEC Cyber Division – Student Selection Questionnaire
Welcome to SPEC Cyber Division! This form checks your curiosity, problem-solving, and dedication. Be honest—mindset matters more than experience. Start your cybersecurity journey today!
The respondent's email (margelu63@gmail.com) was recorded on submission of this form.
* Indicates required question
Section 0: Personal Information 
Full Name *
gelila kefyalew
Id Number: *
ru0930/17
Department *
software engineering
Academic Year *
Section 1: Prior Knowledge Check
2. Do you understand how a program works (basic logic, flow of execution, input/output)? *
1. Do you have experience with any programming language (such as Python, JavaScript, Java, C, C++, C#, or PHP)  *
3. Do you have any basic understanding of how networks or data communication works?
 *
4. Do you know what an operating system is and what it does? *
5. Have you ever heard of or used a virtual machine (VM) *
6. Are you ready to accept that less than 3% of hacking is about tools or coding, and that over 97% requires reading, thinking, and deep understanding—and still stay committed with curiosity and patience? *
Section 2: Motivation & Interest
7. Why do you want to learn cybersecurity?
 *
  • Explain your genuine reason and what drives your interest.
We live so much of our lives online—our finances, our private conversations, and our identities are all stored in code. I want to learn cybersecurity because I believe privacy is a fundamental right. My interest is to help create a digital environment where people can feel safe and secure, knowing their information is protected by robust architecture
Section 3: Self-Learning Ability
8. Have you ever solved a tough problem or learned something complex on your own?
 *
  • Describe the process you followed, challenges you faced, and how you overcame them.
when i took cpp course back in pre engineering i used to practice different problem solving skills by myself like building smth like ATM yes we learned the language as a course but it wasnt much of helping in real world problem so i used to practice it by myself and i actually learned most of it by myself the hardest part was trying to find the efficient algorithm by efficient I mean if we want the code to be clear and neat it would be so long and if we make it short it might not be understandable and easy to update and debug but by repetition it becomes more easier
Section 4: Problem-Solving Skills
9. If you face a problem you don’t understand at all, what steps will you take *
  • Explain your approach step by step
i will spend most of my time in understanding a problem cuz understanding the problem is half way to solution so i will try my best to understand the problem the try to map the mind map for the algorithm then think of different things aboutthe alghorithm like my time or storage or the feasiblity of that solution then start coding then testing
10. Imagine you try something Over 10 times and it keeps failing Even if you tried for Hours. What will you do next?
  • Describe how you respond to repeated failure.
as i said before when i was practicing solving different problems by cpp i used to fail a lot cause i was all by myself so i actually get pretty good experience of keeping up while failing repetitively but i will not be so arrogant i would try to ask someone or may be even use AI or some guidance but i wont be100 percent dependent i actually love doing things my way but i could use a little help
Section 5: Discipline & Focus
11. If this becomes very difficult and slow, will you still continue? Why?
  • Explain your mindset toward long-term challenges.
yes, i will because i have this compulsive obsession thing that i can't let things go unless i finished them so i won't stop unless i did finish it but i am flexible i will try to use different ways but still i won't let that go without getting finished
12. How many hours per day can you realistically dedicate to this club? *
  • Be realistic and specific
3 up to 4 hours it depends on some situation like exam season or something like that but i can manage at least 3 hours per day
